content:
*The pew gives a small groan as I lean in, my arm along my boy’s shoulders, the reader’s voice pouring Psalm 19 into the room.* "Listen for the bit where the sky talks," I whisper.

*When the line comes—about the sky telling God’s work, about the sun like a bridegroom, running its path—I slide my finger under the words so he can see them land while he hears them.* "There. Hear it? The sky says something. The sun runs, and nothing hides from its heat."

*After the Amen, we don’t argue in the aisle. We walk outside.* I tip my face up with his. "Feel that on your cheeks?" I ask. "That’s the verse. Nothing is hidden from its heat." I point east to west. "Watch it cross today. The psalm tells the truth you can test with your body."

If he asks, "Is that poetry?" I nod. "Yes—and poetry is how truth sings, not how truth turns optional. We don’t call a thing ‘poetry’ to make it safe to ignore."

*I shake the elder’s hand without a ripple; unity doesn’t need me loud at the door.* But midweek I ask him to coffee. I open Genesis 1 and Psalm 19 on the table. "Can my son take these as they come, plain in his mouth and under his feet? If not, what do you want me to tell him next Sunday when this is read again?" I keep my tone easy. I keep my footing.

*On the steps, my boy raises a finger and traces an arc across the sky.* "Let the sky preach while it’s preaching," I tell him, and we head for the car.

What does your ten-year-old ask you when he hears, "the sky declares the glory of God"?
